meta.title: Modal opens visually but fails screen-reader users
meta.summary: A custom modal looks fine, but focus escapes behind it and screen readers announce an unnamed generic region.
signals: Screen reader does not announce modal name
Closing the modal loses the original trigger focus

context.symptom: A billing confirmation modal overlays the page, but keyboard users can still tab behind it and VoiceOver announces only 'group'.
context.userImpact: Assistive-technology users lose orientation and cannot complete a destructive action with confidence.
context.environment: Custom React portal modal rendered with divs, close button, and CSS overlay, no dialog primitive underneath.

evidence.title: Keyboard walkthrough
evidence.body: Tab leaves the visible modal and lands on navigation links behind the overlay.

evidence.title: Accessibility tree note
evidence.body: The modal root has no accessible name and no dialog semantics.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: Which diagnosis best fits the accessibility failure?
stages.helperText: Look for the missing semantics and focus behavior, not just visual polish.

options.label: The only issue is that the confirm button text is too short.
options.feedback: Clear copy helps, but it does not explain unnamed dialog output or escaped focus.

options.label: The modal needs a higher z-index so screen readers can detect it.
options.feedback: Z-index is visual. It does not create semantics or keyboard containment.

options.label: This is mostly a VoiceOver bug, so the app should not change much.
options.feedback: The current markup and focus behavior are objectively incomplete; blaming the tool misses the app defect.

options.label: The custom modal is missing the dialog accessibility contract: naming, focus entry, focus return, and background isolation.
options.feedback: Correct. The bug is broader than one aria attribute; the modal never behaves like an actual dialog.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three highest-priority debug checks.
stages.helperText: You need to validate semantics and keyboard behavior before debating implementation details.

candidates.label: Run a keyboard-only open, tab, shift-tab, and escape walkthrough.
Inspect the dialog semantics, accessible name, and aria-modal state.
Verify focus enters the modal on open and returns to the trigger on close.
Tune overlay animation timing to make the modal feel clearer.
Adjust background colors first to improve clarity.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the production fix?
stages.helperText: Choose the updates that make the modal behave like a real dialog for assistive tech and keyboard users.

options.label: Use a dialog primitive or add the correct dialog semantics with an accessible name.
options.feedback: Correct. The modal needs a recognizable dialog role and naming relationship.

options.label: Rewrite placeholders and rely on that as the accessibility improvement.
options.feedback: Helpful copy is not a substitute for dialog semantics and focus management.

options.label: Remove the close button so there are fewer focusable elements to manage.
options.feedback: That makes the modal harder to use and avoids the real accessibility work.

options.label: Move focus into the modal on open, trap it while open, and restore it to the trigger on close.
options.feedback: Correct. Focus lifecycle is central to usable modal behavior.

options.label: Prevent background interaction and tabbing while the modal is open.
options.feedback: Correct. Background content should not compete with the active dialog.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the strongest guardrail after the fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the safeguard that checks behavior, not just markup presence.

options.label: Add an aria linter rule and assume the interaction will stay correct.
options.feedback: Linting helps, but it cannot prove focus movement and background isolation.

options.label: Add an automated modal test that checks keyboard flow, focus return, and accessibility assertions.
options.feedback: Correct. This protects the actual behavior users depend on.

options.label: Replace the design with blocking browser alerts everywhere.
options.feedback: That discards the product UX instead of maintaining an accessible modal implementation.

options.label: Add a screenshot regression because the modal looked right visually.
options.feedback: Visual regression is insufficient for keyboard and screen-reader behavior.

scoreBands.label: Semantics and focus got blurred together
scoreBands.summary: You saw a visual overlay, but not the full dialog contract required for accessibility.

scoreBands.label: Partial accessibility fix
scoreBands.summary: You found key dialog behaviors, but the debug order or guardrail missed one essential behavior.

scoreBands.label: Accessible modal mindset
scoreBands.summary: You treated the modal as a behavioral contract: semantics, focus lifecycle, and background isolation.

idealRunbook: Walk the flow with keyboard first.
Inspect semantics and accessible naming next.
Verify focus entry and return behavior.
Lock the behavior with an automated accessibility regression.

teachingBlocks.title: Modal interview shortcut
teachingBlocks.text: A modal is never just a box on top of the screen. It is a focus-management and semantics problem first.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If you had to choose one manual verification tool here, which one would give the fastest confidence boost?
